nativeRange.habitat: Steep outer reef slopes between 10 and 40 m, typically on the ceilings of caves, overhangs and ledges where individuals are often observed oriented upside down.

diet.notes: Wild diet is undocumented. In captivity, generally advised to offer a varied mix of Spirulina, marine algae sheets, high-quality angelfish preparations, mysis shrimp and other small meaty items. Multiple small feedings per day tend to work better than one or two large ones; established systems with grazable live rock can help reluctant feeders transition.

avoidWith: boisterous tangs or large angels that bully shy fish

summary: A rare, expert-only dwarf angelfish from western Pacific reef caves, notable for its vivid yellow belly and electric-blue back. Reclusive by nature, it tends to become assertive toward similarly sized tankmates as it matures.
careNotes: The Purple Masked Angelfish is regarded as expert-only and is rarely offered in the trade, commanding very high prices when it does appear. Specimens are typically collected from depths of 10 to 40 m and tend to acclimate poorly, so a well-established reef system with abundant live rock, deep caves, and overhangs is generally advised. In the wild, individuals are most often observed oriented upside down on cave ceilings; replicating that structural complexity in the aquarium helps reduce stress. Subdued lighting or a deeply shaded section of rockwork can help shy specimens feed and settle. Reef compatibility is considered cautious — isolated cases of nipping at sessile invertebrates, LPS corals (large-polyp stony corals), and clam mantles have been reported, so close observation is advised in mixed reef systems. Tankmates should be calm, similarly sized or smaller, and unlikely to outcompete the Purple Masked Angelfish at feeding time. Genus placement for this species is unsettled among taxonomists — some treat the subgenus as a full genus and use the name Paracentropyge venusta, while others retain Centropyge venusta; the species was originally described as Holacanthus venustus (Yasuda & Tominaga, 1969). Like other dwarf angelfish, it is generally considered a protogynous hermaphrodite (females that can become males) and is believed to form loose harems on the reef; a single individual or a carefully introduced female-first pair is the safest captive configuration. The species is listed as Least Concern on the IUCN Red List.
breedingNotes: Sporadically bred and reared in captivity, but the species remains exceptionally rare in the hobby and pelagic-spawning protocols are not well documented for hobbyist-scale systems. Like related dwarf angelfish, individuals are believed to be protogynous hermaphrodites that form harems with one dominant male over multiple females. Natural hybrids with Centropyge multifasciata have been reported on the reef.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Source of aquarium husbandry parameters (tank size, temperament, reef compatibility, diet, water parameters). Uses the name Paracentropyge venusta.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (12 cm TL — agrees with LiveAquaria's 5 in / ~12.7 cm), taxonomy (family Pomacanthidae, order Acanthuriformes), depth range (10-40 m), native distribution (Japan to northern Philippines), habitat (steep outer reef slopes, caves and ledges with upside-down orientation), and reproduction (occasional hybrids with C. multifasciata). FishBase retains the genus placement Centropyge venusta (synonym Holacanthus venustus).

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Used to confirm distribution (Ryukyu and Izu Islands, Taiwan, northern Luzon), max size (12 cm), genus/subgenus history (Paracentropyge sometimes treated as distinct genus), aquarium trade scarcity, captive-breeding success, and IUCN Least Concern status.

fish.breedingNotes: The Purple Masked Angelfish is a pelagic spawner, meaning eggs and larvae drift in open water after release. Captive rearing has reportedly been achieved in a small number of cases but is not routine. Like related dwarf angelfish, the species is almost certainly a protogynous hermaphrodite that forms harems on the reef.
